1: A shadow: Jonah's anger at YHWH's mercy to Nineveh — “this greatly displeased Jonah, and he became angry” — is the OT form of the older brother's anger in the Parable of the Prodigal Son (Luke 15:28-30: “he became angry and refused to go in”). Both Jonah and the older brother are members of the covenant community who resent grace extended to the unworthy outsider. Christ embodies the opposite response: “there is more joy in heaven over one sinner who repents than over ninety-nine righteous persons who need no repentance” (Luke 15:7). Jonah's anger about Nineveh's salvation is the anti-shape of Christ's joy over recovered sinners.
2: A revelation of God: Jonah quotes the Exodus 34:6-7 formula — “a gracious God and merciful, slow to anger and abounding in steadfast love, and relenting from disaster” — as the reason for his anger; he resents precisely the character of YHWH that saves him. The irony cuts deep: the chesed (steadfast love) that extended to Nineveh is the same chesed that YHWH extends to Israel and to Jonah. In Christ, this character is expressed completely: “God demonstrates his own love for us in this: while we were still sinners, Christ died for us” (Romans 5:8). The grace Jonah resents for Nineveh is the grace that saves Jonah himself — and it reaches its fullest expression in the cross.
3: A theme: “Now, O LORD, please take away my life, for it is better for me to die than to live.” Jonah's death-wish comes from the unbearable tension of being right about God's character but wrong about where that character should apply. The prophet who preached life to Nineveh wants death for himself. Christ, entering the fullness of human distress in Gethsemane (“My soul is very sorrowful, even to death”, Mark 14:34), does not flee from death but goes through it for others. The same three words — “it is better for me to die” — spoken in self-regarding bitterness by Jonah become in Christ an act of self-giving love: “he humbled himself by becoming obedient to the point of death” (Philippians 2:8).
4: A revelation of God: “Is it right for you to be angry?” YHWH's response to Jonah's death-wish is a question, not a condemnation. The divine pedagogy is patient: instead of rebuking the sulking prophet, YHWH asks a question that invites self-examination. Christ's pedagogy with his disciples follows this same pattern: patient questioning rather than crushing correction (Mark 8:29: “But who do you say that I am?”; John 21:15-17: “Simon, son of John, do you love me?”). The God who asks Jonah whether his anger is just is the God who in Christ asks Peter whether he loves him — both questions aim at the same inner transformation.
5: A theme: Jonah goes east of the city, builds a booth, and sits waiting to see what will happen — watching from a distance for the judgment that YHWH has already decided not to bring. The posture of the one who expects judgment rather than mercy. This is the posture of those who know God's power but struggle to receive his mercy as genuinely good news for others. Christ comes not to watch humanity from a distance but to enter into it completely: “the Word became flesh and dwelt among us” (John 1:14). The booth Jonah builds for himself is the ironic shadow of the tent YHWH pitches in the incarnation.
6: A revelation of God: “The LORD God appointed a plant and made it come up over Jonah to give shade for his head, to save him from his discomfort.” Even in Jonah's anger and sulking, YHWH provides shade and comfort — YHWH cares for the prophet even when the prophet is behaving badly. This is the character Christ describes in Matthew 5:45: “he makes his sun rise on the evil and on the good, and sends rain on the just and on the unjust.” Divine provision is not contingent on human virtue. The plant-provision sets up the pedagogical argument of v10-11: if Jonah values what YHWH provided, how much more does YHWH value the 120,000 people of Nineveh?
7: A revelation of God: “At dawn the next day God appointed a worm, and it attacked the plant so that it withered.” The same God who provided the plant removes it — YHWH controls both provision and its withdrawal. This reveals the God of sovereign pedagogy: every gift is a lesson; every loss is teaching. The NT develops this as the “training” (paideia) of the Father: “the Lord disciplines the one he loves, and chastises every son whom he receives” (Hebrews 12:6, citing Proverbs 3:11-12). What happens to the plant in Jonah is the concrete enactment of what Hebrews 12 says about divine discipline — temporary removal of comfort for permanent transformation.
8: A theme: “The sun beat down on Jonah's head so that he grew faint.” Jonah in the scorching heat, fainting, asking again to die — the physical embodiment of spiritual despair. The prophet who proclaimed the word of God in Nineveh is now incapacitated by grief and heat. Christ enters this same extremity of physical suffering — “I thirst” (John 19:28, citing Psalms 22:15: “my strength is dried up like a potsherd”) — but not in the paralysis of self-pity; in the consummation of self-giving. Both Jonah and Christ suffer; only one's suffering is redemptive.
9: A revelation of God: YHWH's second question — “Do you do well to be angry for the plant?” — and Jonah's defiant answer (“Yes, angry enough to die”) reveals the shape of misplaced value: Jonah is angrier about a plant than about the coming destruction of 120,000 people. The question that God asks is the question that exposes all misplaced priorities. Jesus's teaching ministry is structured around such questions: “What will it profit a man if he gains the whole world and forfeits his soul?” (Matthew 16:26); “Is it lawful to do good or to do harm on the Sabbath, to save life or to destroy it?” (Mark 3:4). The questioner in Jonah 4 is the questioner in the Gospels.
10: A revelation of God: “You pity the plant, for which you did not labor, nor did you make it grow, which came into being in a night and perished in a night.” YHWH's argument from the lesser to the greater: if Jonah pities an uncreated, temporary plant he had no part in making, how much more does YHWH — who made the Ninevites and knows each one — pity them? The same argument structures the parables of Jesus: “If you then, who are evil, know how to give good gifts to your children, how much more will your Father who is in heaven give good things to those who ask him?” (Matthew 7:11). The creator-creature argument reveals the character of the God who sent his only Son (John 3:16) to save what he made.
11: A direct anticipation: “Should I not pity Nineveh, that great city, in which there are more than 120,000 persons who do not know their right hand from their left, and also much cattle?” The book ends here — with God's unanswered question. It is the OT's most direct statement of divine compassion for the ignorant, the helpless, and even the animals of a Gentile city. The NT's answer to this unanswered question is John 3:16: “For God so loved the world that he gave his only Son.” The “world” of John 3:16 is the Nineveh of Jonah 4:11 — vast, ignorant, undeserving, yet the object of divine pity. The Great Commission (Matthew 28:19) is the action that the answer demands: go to all the Ninevehs of the world, because the God who pitied 120,000 Ninevites sent his Son for them all.
